entry.title: Connecting SAP GenAI Hub to n8n: A Complete Guide
entry.content:
A step-by-step tutorial on calling SAP GenAI Hub's large language models from self-hosted n8n to build enterprise AI automation workflows.

SAP Build is the primary, recommended solution for enterprise automation and orchestration within the SAP ecosystem. However, one of the key strengths of SAP GenAI Hub is its standardization—it uses standard REST APIs and OAuth2, meaning it can be consumed by any system, regardless of the tech stack.

This tutorial demonstrates that openness. We will use n8n (a popular self-hosted workflow tool) to show how developers can integrate SAP's enterprise-grade GenAI capabilities into external or hybrid environments for rapid prototyping and testing.

Since n8n doesn't have a native SAP GenAI Hub node yet. No worries — we can easily make the connection using HTTP Request + OAuth2.

Prerequisites

Before we start, make sure you have:

  1. A running n8n instance (Docker or npm or k8s)
  2. SAP BTP account with SAP AI Core service enabled (Extended Plan for GenAI Hub)
  3. A deployed LLM model (configured in SAP AI Launchpad)

 

Get Your SAP AI Core Service Key

Navigate to SAP BTP Cockpit → Your Subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → SAP AI Core → Create Service Key

You'll get a JSON like this:

 
{
  "clientid": "sb-xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx!b12345|aicore!b540",
  "clientsecret": "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx$xxxxxxxxx",
  "url": "https://your-tenant.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com",
  "serviceurls": {
    "AI_API_URL": "https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com"
  }
}

Keep these four fields handy — you'll need them all.

 

Get Your Deployment ID

In SAP AI Launchpad:

  1. Go to ML Operations → Deployments
  2. Find your deployed model
  3. Note the Deployment ID (or the full deploymentUrl)

Step 1: Create OAuth2 Credential

SAP GenAI Hub uses OAuth2 Client Credentials authentication. Let's configure the credential in n8n first.

  1. Open n8n, go to Settings → Credentials
  2. Click Add Credential, search for OAuth2 API
  3. Fill in the following:
Field
Value
Credential NameSAP-GenAI-Hub
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Access Token URLhttps://your-tenant.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
Client IDCopy the full clientid from Service Key
Client SecretCopy the full clientsecret from Service Key
ScopeLeave empty
AuthenticationSelect Header
4. Click Save

💡Common Mistake: Selecting "Send as Basic Auth Header" will cause 401 authentication failures. SAP requires credentials to be sent in the body.

Step 2: Build a Simple Q&A Workflow

Let's create a basic LLM query workflow.

2.1 Add Manual Trigger

Start with a manual trigger for testing:

  • Add a Manual Trigger node

2.2 Add HTTP Request Node

This is the core node. Configure as follows:

Basic Settings:

  • Method: POST
  • URL: 
https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com/v2/inference/deployments/{your_Deployment_ID}/chat/completions​

 ⚠️ Note: If using OpenAI models, you should add the api-version parameter, e.g.

https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com/v2/inference/deployments/{your_Deployment_ID}/chat/completions?api-version=2023-05-15

Authentication:

  • Authentication: Predefined Credential Type
  • Credential Type: OAuth2 API
  • OAuth2 API: Select SAP-GenAI-Hub

Headers:

NameValue
Content-Typeapplication/json
AI-Resource-Groupdefault, or your own Resource Group ID

Body:

  • Body Content Type: JSON
  • Specify Body: Using JSON
{
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "system",
      "content": "You are a professional assistant. Please provide helpful and accurate answers."
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "What is SAP S/4HANA? Please summarize in three sentences."
    }
  ]
}

2.3 Test It

Click Test Workflow. If configured correctly, you'll see a response like:

{
  "choices": [
    {
      "message": {
        "role": "assistant",
        "content": "SAP S/4HANA is SAP's next-generation intelligent ERP suite..."
      }
    }
  ],
  "usage": {
    "prompt_tokens": 45,
    "completion_tokens": 128,
    "total_tokens": 173
  }
}

🎉Congratulations! You've successfully called SAP GenAI Hub from n8n!

Step 3: Build an Interactive Chat Workflow

Let's upgrade to a conversational version.

Workflow Structure

Chat Trigger → HTTP Request (SAP GenAI Hub) → Code (Extract Reply)

3.1 Chat Trigger Node

Add a Chat Trigger node (found under Advanced AI category):

  • Keep default settings
  • It provides an embedded chat interface for testing

3.2 Modify HTTP Request Node

Change the Body to dynamically get user input:

{
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "system",
      "content": "You are a professional enterprise consultant specializing in SAP and digital transformation topics."
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "{{ $json.chatInput }}"
    }
  ]
}

{{ $json.chatInput }} automatically retrieves the user's input from the chat interface.

3.3 Add Code Node to Extract Response

Add a Code node to extract the AI's reply:

const response = $input.all()[0].json;
const aiMessage = response.choices?.[0]?.message?.content || "Sorry, no response received.";

return [{
  json: {
    response: aiMessage  }
}];

3.4 Test the Chat

  1. Activate the Workflow
  2. Click the Chat button to open the chat interface
  3. Start chatting!

Step 4: Add Conversation Memory (Optional)

The above version treats each message independently without context. We can use n8n's Workflow Static Data to implement simple memory.

Improved Workflow

Add a Code node before HTTP Request to manage conversation history:

// Get session ID and user input
const sessionId = $json.sessionId || 'default';
const userInput = $json.chatInput;

// Get history from global static data
const staticData = $getWorkflowStaticData('global');
if (!staticData.conversations) {
  staticData.conversations = {};
}
if (!staticData.conversations[sessionId]) {
  staticData.conversations[sessionId] = [
    {
      role: "system",
      content: "You are a professional enterprise consultant specializing in SAP and digital transformation topics."
    }
  ];
}

// Add user message
staticData.conversations[sessionId].push({
  role: "user",
  content: userInput});

// Keep only the last 20 messages (avoid token limit)
const messages = staticData.conversations[sessionId].slice(-20);

return [{
  json: {
    sessionId,
    messages  }
}];

Then modify the HTTP Request Body:

{
  "messages": {{ JSON.stringify($json.messages) }}
}

Finally, save the assistant's reply in the response extraction Code node:

const response = $input.all()[0].json;
const sessionId = $('Prepare Messages').first().json.sessionId;
const aiMessage = response.choices?.[0]?.message?.content || "Sorry, no response received.";

// Save assistant reply to history
const staticData = $getWorkflowStaticData('global');
staticData.conversations[sessionId].push({
  role: "assistant",
  content: aiMessage});

return [{
  json: {
    response: aiMessage  }
}];

Now your chatbot remembers context!

Practical Use Cases

Use Case 1: Automated Email Replies

Email Trigger → Extract Content → SAP GenAI Hub → Send Reply

Sample Prompt:

 
Based on the following customer email, generate a professional reply:
Email content:{{ $json.emailBody }}
Requirements:1. Professional and friendly tone2. If it's a price inquiry, mention that detailed pricing will follow within 24 hours3. If it's a complaint, express apology and commitment to follow up

Use Case 2: Document Summarization

Webhook → Read PDF → SAP GenAI Hub → Save to Notion

 

Use Case 3: Data Analysis Assistant

Combine SAP APIs to fetch business data and let AI generate analysis reports:

Schedule Trigger → SAP S/4HANA → SAP GenAI Hub → Slack Message

Troubleshooting

❌401 Unauthorized

Cause: Authentication failed

Check:

  • Client ID and Secret are fully copied (including special characters like !, |, $)
  • Authentication is set to Send In Body
  • Token URL format is correct (ends with /oauth/token)

❌404 Not Found

Cause: API path error

Check:

  • Deployment ID is correct
  • Model is successfully deployed (status shows "Running" in AI Launchpad)
  • API URL path format: /v2/inference/deployments/{id}/chat/completions

❌403 Forbidden

Cause: Insufficient permissions

Check:

  • AI-Resource-Group header is set (default is default)
  • User has permission to access the deployment

❌400 Bad Request

Cause: Invalid request body

Check:

  • JSON format is correct
  • messages is an array
  • Each message has both role and content

Optimization Tips

  1. Limit conversation history: Keep only the last N turns to avoid exceeding context window
  2. Add error handling: Use n8n's Error Trigger to handle API failures gracefully
  3. Use variables: Store API URLs and Deployment IDs in n8n Variables for easier management

Summary

In this tutorial, you learned:

✅How to configure OAuth2 authentication for SAP GenAI Hub in n8n
✅How to use HTTP Request node to call LLM APIs
✅How to build a chatbot with conversation memory
✅How to troubleshoot common errors

The power of n8n lies in its flexibility — you can seamlessly integrate SAP GenAI Hub with hundreds of other applications (Email, Databases, SAP S/4HANA, etc.) to build truly enterprise-grade AI automation workflows.

Happy Automating! 🚀

entry.title: On the RPT-1 podcast: practical AI for SAP data (demos, lessons, and what’s next) 🎙️
entry.content:
If you work in SAP, you already know the truth: our world is rows, columns, master data, transactional history, and process context — not creative writing, in other words - SAP is not a “write me a poem” platform. That’s why I really enjoyed this conversation on SAP RPT-1, where we went beyond “AI hype” and into what’s genuinely useful for SAP practitioners.

RPT-1 is built for structured enterprise data. In plain terms: it’s designed to reason over the kinds of datasets we live in every day—think finance, supply chain, procurement, operations—where accuracy, traceability, and consistency matter more than fancy wording.

What you’ll get from the episode

  • A practical view of “AI for SAP data” — where it fits (and where it doesn’t)

  • Real demos and prototypes focused on outcomes, not theory

  • How to think about predictive + analytical use cases that start small but scale (without turning into a 6-month science project)

  • A clear message: move from chatbot experiments to measurable business impact

Why this matters for the SAP community

Most AI discussions ignore a key point: SAP value is locked in structured data + process discipline. The moment you align AI with that reality—tabular signals, business semantics, governance—you shift from “cool demo” to real operational leverage.

If you’re exploring AI for SAP and you want something that’s grounded in enterprise reality (data quality, governance, repeatability, outcomes), this episode is a solid watch:

🎥Watch here my podcast with Holger and Goran from Microsoft:
https://youtu.be/CbUDRgEO0yI?si=J4vE-BZ0Xig_srYo



Please share your thoughts and feedback! 

Cheers,
Amit Lal

entry.title: Decision Logic Modernization in SAP Integrations: Using SAP-RPT-1 for Intelligent Routing Decisions
entry.content:
Integration Modernization concept is often discussed in two dimensions:
1) Platform modernization – moving from older stacks like SAP NetWeaver PI/PO, SAP Neo, or legacy 3rd party platforms to SAP Integration Suite.
2) Scenario modernization – shifting from legacy protocols to modern integration patterns such as APIs & event-driven architectures; upgrading ABAP / Java mapping to graphical mapping / groovy script; or moving from basic auth to OAuth 2.0 or client certificate based authentication, and so on.

But there is another Modernization opportunity at the scenario level: decision logic modernization.

In this blog, we explore how routing decision logic, as an orchestration step — can be modernized using SAP-RPT-1, SAP’s latest foundation model hosted on BTP AI Core (Generative AI Hub) — applied to an outbound delivery routing scenario.

The Traditional Approach: Static Rules, Lookups, and Growing Complexity:
In SAP PI/PO and Integration Suite, routing decisions are typically implemented using deterministic, rule-based logic. These decisions often control how a scenario behaves – including which receiver system to call, or which transformation branch to follow. The common approaches include:

  • Static routing via Routers based on payload elements or fields
  • Dynamic configurations, such as externalized parameters passed at runtime
  • Lookups to external systems (e.g., via JDBC, SOAP, RFC lookups) to evaluate conditions at runtime — achieved in Integration Suite via patterns like Request-Reply or Content Enricher

These mechanisms work — but they come with growing challenges:

  • Rules get combinatorially complex with more conditions and evolving business scenarios
  • Lookup tables become burdensome to maintain
  • Decisions become rigid and hard to evolve over time

As a result, rule-based decisioning becomes harder to maintain as business requirements evolve. Agility suffers. Adaptation becomes expensive.

What if, instead of relying on static rule-based logic, iflows could make decisions based on historical business patterns — applying predictive machine learning without the overhead of training and managing custom models? With the in-context learning capabilities of SAP’s latest foundation model, SAP-RPT-1, such predictions can be performed on-the-fly at runtime.

Enter SAP-RPT-1: Foundation Model for Predictive Tasks:
SAP-RPT-1 is a pre-trained foundation model available via BTP AI Core (Generative AI Hub). Unlike traditional ML services that require dedicated data science setup, SAP-RPT-1 offers:

  • Pre-trained model, ready for “plug and play” in your scenario
  • No ML infrastructure setup
  • No data science expertise required
  • No-code consumption via API
  • Faster time-to-market
  • Democratization of AI capabilities across IT functions

SAP-RPT-1 is pre-trained specifically for predictive tasks such as classification and regression over ERP tabular data, unlike many general-purpose GenAI models that are primarily trained on large corpora of natural language text. Instead of building and managing a full ML lifecycle, integration developers can now leverage predictive intelligence directly into iflows — without any data science related heavy-lifting.

If you are new to SAP-RPT-1, I recommend that you read this introductory blog.
Also, explore this SAP-RPT-1 playground.

Business Scenario: Intelligent Outbound Delivery Routing with SAP-RPT-1:
SmartSense Technologies (SST), a global provider of smart security devices, faces challenges in routing outbound deliveries from its manufacturing plants to distribution centers, retail stores, and end customers. Their current routing logic, embedded in integration flows, relies on static rules and lookup tables – employing a combination of Material, Quantity, Delivery priority, Material group, Ship to location, and so on. This leads to frequent shipping delays, cost overruns, and SLA violations whenever logistics conditions change and sub-optimal 3PL partner is chosen.

To overcome these limitations, SST decides to leverage SAP Integration Suite together with SAP-RPT-1 foundation model. By embedding machine learning–driven decision-making into its integration flow, SST aims to dynamically select the optimal logistics partner for each delivery based on historical performance, cost, and delivery times. While SAP-RPT-1 is capable of solving predictive tasks such as classification and regression, we will be using it for classification task — i.e. to classify given delivery (defined by delivery header & item attributes) to most optimal 3PL logistics provider.

The following diagram illustrates the target architecture implemented by SST for intelligent outbound delivery routing using SAP Integration Suite and SAP-RPT-1 model, hosted on BTP’s Generative AI hub:


Step 1: Outbound Delivery Creation
An outbound delivery is created in SAP S/4HANA, which triggers an outbound notification event. The event is consumed by the iflow, which then calls S/4HANA OData API to fetch the following delivery related header and item attributes.

Delivery header fields: DeliveryDate, SalesOrganization, ShipToParty, SoldToParty, DeliveryDocumentType, CreationDate, ShippingPoint, DeliveryPriority, IncotermsClassification, TransactionCurrency

Delivery item fields: Material, DeliveryDocumentItemText, MaterialGroup, ActualDeliveryQuantity, DeliveryQuantityUnit, Plant, StorageLocation, ItemGrossWeight, ItemWeightUnit, ControllingArea, DistributionChannel, GoodsMovementType, ProfitCenter

 

Step 2: Context Selection – Random Historical Records
We store historic labeled data i.e. delivery headers, items and corresponding optimal 3PL partner data in SAP HANA Cloud. The iflow is configured to fetch a random set of this historic data from HANA Cloud. As recommended in this blog, for most enterprise use-cases, where you have about hundred thousand to million lines of labelled data, depending on how diverse your scenario data is, you may choose the context size, by iterating and finalizing the apt number of historical records to pick. By picking random records, we are able to provide statistically diverse examples, improve prediction quality and avoid bias from sequential records.

The Iflow executes the following query on the HANA Cloud DB:

SELECT TOP ${property.NumberofRecords}
"DeliveryDate",   "DeliveryDocumentNumber",  "CreationDate", "DeliveryPriority",  "IncotermsClassification",  "Material", "DeliveryDocumentItemText", "MaterialGroup",  "ActualDeliveryQuantity", "DeliveryQuantityUnit", "ItemGrossWeight", "ItemWeightUnit", "DistributionChannel", "PartnerNumber"
FROM "RPT1"."historic_3pl_selections"
ORDER BY RAND()


Step 3: Constructing the SAP-RPT-1 Request
We now combine:

  • Historical random records (known 3PL partner)
  • The current outbound delivery (3PL partner to be predicted)

The current transaction includes a placeholder: [PREDICT]

The request payload contains:

  • 900 historical labeled rows
  • 1 new outbound delivery row marked for prediction

For detailed info on payload construction, refer to the official documentation page and the GitHub repository (sample Postman/Bruno collections available for experimentation).

Further, while creating the inferencing request to SAP-RPT-1, we trim the columns from original 24 down to only 14 columns of delivery attributes, by removing fields like Sales Org, Controlling Area, Profit centre that do not influence partner selection. This is one of the best practices to achieve best possible predictive outputs. Read more recommended best practices here.
 

Step 4: Deploying SAP-RPT-1 model in Generative AI Hub


Step 5: SAP-RPT-1 inference response
The response contains Number of predicted records, predicted field (here optimal 3PL partner) along with Confidence score.

Response from our example scenario:

You can also send multiple unknown records in one batch. To read more about the number of max. records and columns supported by SAP-RPT-1, check out this help page.

Step 6: Intelligent Routing in the Iflow
The Iflow now:

  1. Reads the predicted PartnerNumber
  2. Evaluates confidence threshold (optional safeguard)
  3. Routes outbound delivery to the optimal 3PL partner
  4. Optionally, logs decision + confidence for traceability in HANA Cloud.


When AI in Iflows Brings Real Business Value

  • When routing decisions directly impact business outcomes
    If sub-optimal routing can lead to SLA breaches, shipment delays, higher costs, or customer dissatisfaction, intelligent decision logic creates measurable advantage.
  • When rule-based logic becomes complex and brittle
    As business variables multiply (products, regions, priorities, partners), static rules become hard to maintain and scale. ML handles multi-dimensional patterns more effectively.
  • When historical ERP data contains predictive patterns
    If past delivery and fulfillment data reflects consistent business behavior, ML can learn from it and improve future routing decisions beyond deterministic conditions.
  • When business context evolves over time
    With changing product mixes, customer bases, or distribution models, retrainable ML models adapt dynamically — avoiding constant manual rule maintenance.

Not every integration scenario requires ML capabilities. But where routing decisions carry operational or financial impact, replacing rigid rule sets with adaptive decision logic can significantly improve resilience and maintainability. Decision logic modernization is therefore not about adding AI everywhere — it is about applying it where it meaningfully improves orchestration.

Note: This blog revisits our earlier implementation in light of the planned deprecation of SAP BTP Data Attribute Recommendation service, demonstrating an updated approach using SAP-RPT-1 model.

entry.title: Joule Studio: Documentation for Document Grounding and Testing, Deploying & Launch Joule Agent
entry.content:
Note: Enablement of Joule Studio (IMPORTANT): Joule Studio Enablement Guide

📑 Table of Contents:

  1. Introduction
  2. What Is Document Grounding in Joule Studio?
  3. Architecture Overview
  4. Prerequisites
      SAP Services Requirements
      Access Requirements
      Required Credentials from Service Keys
  5. Setting Up Destinations
      AI Core Destination
      Object Store Destination
  6. Configuring Document Grounding in Control Tower
      Create Resource Group
  7. Monitoring Status
  8. AWS S3 CLI Guide for Uploading Documents for Grounding
  9. Viewing Grounding Pipelines in SAP AI Launchpad
  10. Adding Document Grounding to Joule Studio Agents
  11. Using Joule Agent - Test in Private Environment and Deploy & Launch Joule Agent
  12. Troubleshooting

1. Introduction

Document Grounding is a capability in Joule Studio that lets you attach custom documents (PDFs, manuals, SOPs, policies, knowledge bases) to an AI agent.
This ensures the agent gives highly accurate, contextually relevant, and source-backed answers.

In older setups, admins had to manually manage:

  • Generic Secrets
  • Base64 encoding
  • Pipelines in SAP AI Launchpad

Now, Joule Studio automates these tasks through Control Tower, simplifying the job for developers.

 

2. What Is Document Grounding in Joule Studio?

Document Grounding performs these functions:

  1. Reads documents stored in an S3-compatible Object Store
  2. Creates a grounding pipeline inside SAP AI Core
  3. Creates a Resource Group to store the pipeline + embeddings
  4. Makes the resource available to Joule Studio Agents

This allows the agent to:

  • Retrieve answers strictly from the grounded documents
  • Reduce hallucinations
  • Use enterprise-verified content

3. Architecture Overview

Key interactions:

  • Control Tower creates Resource Groups
  • AI Core runs embedding pipelines
  • Object Store provides documents
  • Joule Studio binds the grounding data to agents

 

4. Prerequisites

You must have:

SAP Services

  • SAP AI Core (INSTANCE - Extended Plan)
  • SAP AI Launchpad (APPLICATION - Standard Plan)
  • Object Store (INSTANCE - s3-standard or standard)
  • SAP Build Control Tower Access 
  • SAP Build Lobby with Joule Studio enabled (Joule Studio Enablement Guide)
  • Add all the roles to the user from BTP Cockpit → Security → User → (select the user(s)) → Role Collections → Assign Role Collections → Select all the roles related to that (SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, Object Store, and SAP Build )

NOTE: For Object Store plan Standard, make 2 keys with Role "Read" and "Write".

 



Access Requirements 

  • Admin role for Control Tower
  • Developer role for Joule Studio
  • Object Store admin or contributor
  • AI Core admin or contributor

Required Credentials from Service Keys

  • AI Core Service Key
  • Object Store Service Key

5. Setting Up Destinations

Two destinations must be configured:
1. AI Core Destination
2. Object Store Destination
These allow Joule Studio to perform grounding operations behind the scenes.

AI Core Destination

Step 1 — Retrieve AI Core Service Key

From BTP → Instance → Service Key

Important fields:

  • url
  • clientid
  • clientsecret
  • tokenurl (must modify)

Step 2 — Configure Destination

Destination Type: HTTP

Field

Value

URL

<AI Core Service Key URL>

Client ID

clientid

Client Secret

clientsecret

Token Service URL

<AI Core URL>/oauth/token (must append /oauth/token)

Proxy Type

Internet

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Step 3 — Add Additional Properties

These ensure visibility in Joule Studio.

Property

Value

sap-joule-studio-document-grounding-ai-core

 true

Important Note

If you forget to append /oauth/token, the grounding pipeline will not trigger.

 

Object Store Destination

Step 1 — Retrieve Object Store Service Key


Note: For Standard Plan, Use the Read role key for the destination.

Extract values such as:

  • access_key_id
  • secret_access_key
  • host
  • bucket
  • region

Step 2 — Configure Destination

Destination Field

Service Key Field

URL

host (add https://)

Client ID

access_key_id

Client Secret

secret_access_key

Token Service URL

host (add https://)

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Proxy Type

Internet

Step 3 — Additional Properties

Property

Value

region

region from service key

bucket

bucket value

username

username from service key

type

S3

sap-joule-studio-document-grounding-object-store

true

These properties ensure that Joule Studio can detect the Object Store.

 

 

6. Configuring Document Grounding in Control Tower

Control Tower now includes a Joule module with Document Grounding options.

Admins decide:

  • Which Object Store to use
  • Which AI Core instance will create pipelines

6.1 Create Resource Group

Steps:

  1. Go to SAP Build Control Tower
  2. Navigate:
    Control Tower → Joule → Document Grounding
  3. Click Add
  4. Provide a Resource Group Name
  5. Select the AI Core Destination
  6. Select the Object Store Destination
  7. Click Create

A new resource group is created and grounding begins automatically.

 

7. Monitoring Status

Resource group states explained:

Status

Meaning

Pending

Pipeline running; embeddings not finished

Completed

Successfully grounded

Error

Incorrect destination or corrupted document

Troubleshooting:

  • Check destination credentials
  • Ensure documents are not corrupted
  • Ensure Object Store bucket name matches

Note:

After approximately few minute (depending on the size of the documents), the status will change to Completed.

In earlier instances, error messages occasionally appeared, or the status remained Pending for an extended period. At times, the status alternated between these messages. In such cases, it is important to ensure that the destinations are configured correctly and that the documents in the Object Store are not corrupted.

 

8. AWS S3 CLI Guide for Uploading Documents for Grounding

1. Install AWS CLI

Run the following command:

brew install awscli

2. Configure AWS CLI

After installation, open Command Prompt (Windows) or Terminal (Mac/Linux) and configure the AWS CLI using your AWS credentials.


Note: For Standard Plan, Use the Write role key for the uploading the documents.

Run the following command:

aws configure

You will be prompted to enter:

  • AWS Access Key ID
  • AWS Secret Access Key
  • Default region name (for example: eu-central-1)
  • Default output format (for example: json, or press Enter to leave it as default)

3. Upload a Grounding Document to AWS S3

To upload a local file to an S3 bucket, use the following command:

aws s3 cp <local-file-path> s3://<bucket-name>/

Example:

aws s3 cp user/…/LA.pdf s3://my-grounding-bucket/

4. Verify File Upload

To confirm that the file was uploaded successfully, list the contents of the S3 bucket:

Note: For Standard plan user again need to configure AWS CLI with read key or you can use Cyberduck for checking the uploaded docs and AWS CLI for uploading.

aws s3 ls s3://<bucket-name>/

If the file appears in the list, the upload was successful.

9. Viewing Grounding Pipelines in SAP AI Launchpad

Once grounding is triggered:

  1. Go to SAP AI Launchpad
  2. Add API Connection
  3. Once loaded, go to: Workspaces → Select the resource Group

    Note: Wait for few minutes until, the resource group is created.


  4. Navigate:
    AI Core Administration → Resource Groups
  5. You will see a new group named:
    {ResourceGroupName}{UnixTimestamp}

  6. Select/Click the resource group, the go to
    Generative AI Hub → Grounding Management → Click on pipeline created.
    This confirms grounding is complete.

    Note: If the pipeline is showing "This data repository is currently empty", then check the destination and s3 bucket.

 

10. Adding Document Grounding to Joule Studio Agents

Developers now consume the resource group.

Steps:

  1. Project Creation: Open Joule Studio → Lobby → Create button → Joule Agent and Skill / Joule Skill
    Give Project Name and Description. Click on the project

  2. Create Joule Agent: Create → Joule Agent

  3. Fill all the necessary details carefully and meaningfully. 
  4. Navigate to Tools Section → Add Tools → Documents


  5. Provide:
    • Tool Name
    • Description
  6. Select one of the available Admin-created Resource Groups

The agent now has grounding enabled.

 

11. Using Joule Studio - Test in Private Environment and Deploy & Launch Joule Agent

  1. Save the Agent.
  2. Make sure Private Environment for testing must be activated: Control Tower → Tenant Configuration (Environments) → Click on Private Environment Active.
  3. Enable Generative AI: Control Tower → Tenant Configuration (Generative AI) → Enable Generative AI.
  4. Enable Generative AI: Control Tower → Tenant Configuration (AI Agent Builder Activation) → Enable Agent Builder.
  5. Joule Agent Window → Test → Select Private → Continue.


  6. Test is woking


  7. Release the Joule Agent


  8. Make a Environment for deploying the Agent: Control Tower → Environments → Create 


  9. Deploy to created environment.


     

  10. Launch: Control Tower → Environments → Select the created environment agent_test_env → More (Joule) → Launch 


    You can use the link as per your preference.


12. Troubleshooting

Common issues:
- Wrong destination credentials
- Missing '/oauth/token'
- Corrupted PDF files in Object Store
- Resource group stuck in Pending

entry.title: Practical Lessons in the Development of Advanced RAG Architectures in SAP Business AI
entry.content:
The Challenge

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) has quickly become the backbone of modern enterprise AI. In the SAP ecosystem—where business processes rely on massive, complex, and varied documents—RAG isn't just a buzzword. It's a critical layer of contextual intelligence that makes corporate data actually usable.

When you get into real-world projects, you realize RAG is about much more than just building conversational chatbots. We’re talking about deep support assistants that can handle complex business workflows using unstructured data. Think along the lines of:

  • Assistants tackling highly technical documentation queries

  • Automated document validation in Procurement

  • Verification of mandatory contractual clauses

  • Intelligent support for financial and logistics operations

  • Contextual analysis of internal procedures and policies

In these scenarios, a "plausible" answer isn't good enough. The business needs precise, traceable, and reliable responses that meet strict enterprise standards. Moving from a flashy demo to a scalable, production-ready solution doesn't just depend on picking a good generative model. It comes down to two structural factors:

  • Ingestion quality (grounding): How we transform raw content into meaningful units that can be retrieved accurately.

  • Relevance of retrieved content (retrieval): The quality of the context we feed the LLM so it can generate the right answer.

This is exactly why a proof-of-concept might look great on day one, but fall apart in production. If your ingestion breaks document structure, or your retrieval misses the key details, the LLM is flying blind—leading to incomplete answers, confusion, and hallucinations. The real challenge isn't just technical; it's architectural and strategic.

 

There Is No Single “Winning” Architecture: The Toolbox Matters

A common trap when approaching RAG is searching for the one "perfect" combination of chunking, embeddings, vector stores, and LLMs. The reality? The right stack depends entirely on your use case.

Because of this, our team at AYESA didn't just try to guess the final architecture. Instead, we treated this as a rigorous process: comparing techniques side-by-side and measuring their real-world impact. The real value of this three-month exercise was figuring out:

  • Which technique actually adds value in a specific context

  • The hidden costs associated with each setup

  • The risks introduced by different methods (like loss of structure, added noise, or semantic degradation)


Starting Point: Quantitative Evaluation Framework

To cut through the noise and avoid subjective opinions ("this answer looks okay"), we set up a strict evaluation framework using RAGAS (https://docs.ragas.io). We built a control dataset of 11 highly complex technical questions alongside their ideal answers (Ground Truth).

We weighted our metrics to explicitly balance retrieval and generation, giving us a reliable final score:

  • Context Recall (40%): Does the retrieved context actually contain the necessary information? (This is critical).
  • Faithfulness (20%): Is the answer based strictly on the context, or is the model hallucinating
  • Answer Relevancy (20%): Does the answer actually address the user's intent?
  • Context Precision (20%): Does the most relevant information show up at the top of the search results?

To keep everything secure and governed, all generative interactions with the LLM were orchestrated through SAP AI Launchpad.

Evolution Sequence: From V1 to V7

During our research, we documented seven iterative versions. We defined each version by pairing a specific Grounding strategy (G) with a Retrieval strategy (R). You can see how our global score evolved here:

 

Here is a breakdown of our journey: the techniques, the tools, and why we got the results we did. Our base architecture relied on SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, and HANA Cloud.

 

V1 - Simple RAG (Baseline)

Technique:

  • Grounding (G1): fixed-size document chunking
  • Retrieval (R1): similarity search in SAP HANA Cloud, retrieving the top 3 chunks

Baseline score: 0.73

Value and Applicability: V1 is basically our minimum viable architecture. It’s great for getting a quick baseline and validating the end-to-end pipeline (ingestion, embeddings, vector store, retrieval, and generation).

Why It Falls Short: When dealing with complex documentation, fixed chunking is brutal—it breaks tables in half and separates closely tied concepts. Also, retrieving only 3 fragments limits your context recall, especially for questions that require the LLM to synthesize interconnected information.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V2 - Parent/Child Strategy

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Small “child” chunks for precise searching, mapped to larger “parent” chunks that preserve the full context.

  • Retrieval (R2): We search against the children, but pass the parents to the LLM for the final context.

Score: 0.78 👍

Value: This is a lifesaver when documents have strict hierarchies (sections, subsections, annexes). It boosts search precision without destroying the semantic flow.

Why It Improves: By retrieving the “parent,” the LLM isn't forced to guess based on isolated, out-of-context fragments. V2 gave us a much better balance between precision and having enough context to work with.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V3 - LLM-Generated Chunks and Keywords

Technique

  • Grounding (G3): LLM-generated chunks and keywords
  • Retrieval (R3): similarity search + keyword-based reordering

Score: 0.60 👎👎 (worse than V2)

Why It Failed Here:
Using an LLM for chunking introduced unexpected risks:

  • The model created segments that didn't match the actual semantic boundaries of the text.

  • The generated keywords often failed to accurately represent the highly technical domain. In this case, trying to add more "intelligence" actually degraded the quality.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V4 - Multi-Query + Reranker #1 (MS MARCO)

Technique

  • Grounding (G2):Back to the Parent/Child strategy.
  • Retrieval (R4): Generate 3 query variants and rerank the resulting chunks using MS MARCO.

Score: 0.66 👍

Why Improvement Was Limited: MS MARCO is a great general-purpose reranker, but that’s exactly the problem. In domains heavily loaded with SAP-specific terminology, it tends to reorder chunks based on generic semantics that misalign with the technical intent.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V5 - Reranker #2 (BGE)

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Parent/Child
  • Retrieval (R5): A broad search (Top 10) followed by specialized reranking using BGE, keeping the Top 3.

Score: 0.83 👍👍 (significant jump)

Why It Works: Casting a wider net during the initial retrieval dramatically improves Context Recall, while the specialized reranking fixes Context Precision. This one-two punch is highly effective for production-grade enterprise assistants.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V6 - Architecture with Cohere

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Parent/Child
  • Retrieval (R6): broad search (Top 10) + Cohere commercial reranker, keeping top 3.

Score: 0.84 👍 and Context Recall 95% 👍

Value: This setup maximizes coverage without overwhelming the LLM with useless context. Commercial rerankers like Cohere add a layer of robustness and generalization that is hard to beat.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V7 -  Final Architecture (SAP HANA Native Cross-Encoder)

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Parent/Child
  • Retrieval (R7): We swapped the third-party reranker for SAP’s native Cross-Encoder (December 2025).

Score: 0.87 👍  and Context Recall 100% 👍 👍 👍

Analysis: This was the game-changer. By using a reranker specifically trained and aligned with SAP’s enterprise domain, we maxed out our precision. Hitting 100% recall proved we had finally eliminated our retrieval blind spots.

 

Conclusions, Lessons Learned, and Best Practices

Ingestion Is Not a Detail: It Is Central Design

  • V1 → V2 confirms naive chunking is insufficient. Parent/Child acts as a quality safeguard.
  • Best practice: Preserve your hierarchies, tables, and dependencies. Do not just chunk text—chunk meaning.

Generalist Components May Fail in Technical Domains

  • V4 was a harsh reminder that generic rerankers won't necessarily beat strategies designed to respect domain specifics.
  • Best practice: Prioritize models aligned with your technical terminology, and measure everything with frameworks like RAGAS.

Winning Pattern: Broad Retrieval + Specialized Reranking

  • The progression from V5 to V7 proved this is the most reliable enterprise strategy.
  • Best practice: Investing time and resources into a good reranker is almost always more profitable than endlessly "prompt tuning" your LLM.

Explicit Metrics Prevent Subjective Debates

  • RAGAS gave us the objective truth. Weighting recall so heavily reflects a practical reality: if the model doesn't have the right context, it cannot give a safe answer.
  • Best practice: Define what "quality" means for your project upfront (faithfulness, recall, precision, relevance) and stick to it.

Alignment with the SAP Ecosystem

  • V7 proved that leaning into native SAP components not only simplifies the architecture but delivers superior performance.

Final Conclusion

This three-month deep dive by the SAP Business AI team at AYESA proves that excellence in enterprise RAG doesn't come from finding a "magic model." It comes from iterative architecture and ruthless, rigorous evaluation. Our evolution to V7 provides a solid blueprint for architects looking to deploy reliable, high-performance assistants on SAP #BTP.

 

@ChristophMorgen @mfath @mkemeter @thiago

entry.title: SAP Build Work Zone: Tile Opens(content federation) but App is opening blank in Mobile
entry.content:
I am currently integrating a custom role and tile from an on‑premise S/4HANA system into SAP Build Work Zone using the CDM-based content federation (Exposure Version 2).

Everything works fine in Build Work Zone in the browser.
However, when I scan the QR code in SAP Start mobile app, the tile appears correctly — but after tapping the tile, the app opens a blank white screen.

 

 

Has anyone experienced this issue where a tile loads correctly in Build Work Zone, but shows a blank screen when launched from SAP Start?

entry.title: Incident Management with AI Adapter and ServiceNow in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction:

With the latest capabilities in SAP Integration Suite, organizations can make incident handling much smarter and faster. By combining the AI Adapter with the ServiceNow Adapter, integration flows can automatically analyze errors and create incident tickets without human intervention.

Instead of developers or support teams manually reviewing logs and raising tickets, Generative AI can interpret the error, understand the issue, and automatically trigger the creation of incident in ServiceNow. This makes the entire exception-handling process more efficient and responsive.

By introducing AI into the integration workflow, organizations can significantly reduce manual effort and lower the Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) for critical integration failures.

 

Disclaimer: This blog includes extensive step-by-step explanations and several screenshots, which may make the article longer than usual. 

 

Concept Overview:

The architecture follows three simple steps:

1. Capture the Error Messages

It’s best to create a generic iflow to handle the entire process of automating the ticket creation in ServiceNow from the error generated in other iflows to be directed using JMS or processDirect adapters

2. Analyze and Generate the Prompt

Using the AI Adapter, the error payload is sent to a Large Language Model (LLM). The model analyzes the stack trace or error message and generates:

  • A simplified explanation of the issue
  • Possible root causes
  • Suggested resolution steps

3. Raise Ticket using ServiceNow Adapter

The enriched information is then sent to the ServiceNow Adapter, which automatically creates a structured incident ticket containing the AI-generated analysis.

AI Adapter Variants:

The AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite currently supports two variants:

  1. AICore-GenAIHub
  2. Custom-OpenAI-REST

AICore-GenAIHub

The AICore-GenAIHub variant integrates with the SAP AI Core service via the SAP Generative AI Hub. SAP GenAI Setup

The Generative AI Hub provides a central gateway to multiple generative AI models from different providers, allowing organizations to consume and manage AI models through SAP’s AI infrastructure on BTP. This enables us to switch between

To use this variant:

  • SAP AI Core service must be configured in the BTP tenant.
  • It supports OAuth2 Client Credentials authentication
  • The service is available only in enterprise or extended environments, not in trial accounts.

Since this setup is not available in the trial landscape, this article will focus on the Custom-OpenAI-REST variant.

Custom-OpenAI-REST

The Custom-OpenAI-REST variant allows integration with AI models that are not hosted or managed by SAP.

This option is used when an AI platform exposes OpenAI-compatible APIs but is not directly supported by SAP’s predefined adapter configurations.

Key characteristics:

  • Configuration Type: Basic or Advanced
  • Connects to OpenAI-compatible endpoints
  • Uses standard REST APIs such as
    • /chat/completions
    • /responses
  • Supports API Key based authentication using Secure Parameters

This enables integration with external AI providers such as OpenAI, Google, or other platforms that expose OpenAI-style APIs.

Prerequisites:

Before building this integration scenario, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  1. Google AI Studio account

    Choose an AI model and generate an API key.

    In this example, we will use the Gemini 1.5 Flash model available via Google AI Studio.

  2. ServiceNow account and Instance

    A ServiceNow account and an active instance are required to create and manage incident tickets through the ServiceNow adapter.

Note: Since I’m using the Gemini AI model, I will configure the adapter using the Advanced configuration type. I will also explain the Basic configuration type and highlight the key differences between the two.

Subscribe to the Gemini Flash Model

First, log in to Google AI Studio and generate an API key that will be used in our IFlow.

After successfully logging in, navigate to the Dashboard section to proceed with the API key creation.

 

Next, go to the Projects section and create a new project by providing an appropriate name. Navigate to the Keys section and create a new key.

Click on the Create API key

Your key has now been generated. Make sure to save it, as it will be required later.

Alternatively, you can click on “Copy cURL quickstart”, which will provide the complete URL, API key, and request body, as shown below.

curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-flash-latest:generateContent" \
-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
-H 'X-goog-api-key: <Your API Key>' \
-X POST \
-d '{
"contents": [
{
"parts": [
{
"text": "Explain how AI works in a few words"
}
]
}
]
}'

ServiceNow Instance Creation:

Now let’s set up the ServiceNow Developer account and create an instance. Visit the ServiceNow Developers page and create an account using your e-mail address. Once you verify your e-mail, you will be redirected to the following screen.

Select the instance of your choice. In my case, I’m selecting the Zurich instance.

Your personal developer instance has now been created, and you will be able to see the generated username and password. We will use these credentials in the ServiceNow Adapter for Basic Authentication

Now that we have the credentials, we need the API details to create an incident in ServiceNow. Log in to your instance using the REST API Explorer | ServiceNow. Once you open it, you will be able to see the list of available resources.

  1. API Name: Select Table API.
  2. Choose the Create a Record (POST) option.
  3. Copy the URL and replace {tablename} with the actual table name (in our case, the incident table).
  4. Scroll down and select the fields that need to be included in the request body.

Note: In ServiceNow, all data is stored in tables. It is also possible to create an empty incident without passing any data in the request body.

As shown below, a ticket is created even when no data is provided in the request body.

To view the list of created incidents, go to Workspaces, search for Incidents, select All Results, and under Service Decks, click on Incidents.

IFlow Process Flow Overview

Finally, let’s take a look at our IFlow and understand how we will use the above setup in our adapters.

Integration Flow

As the Sender, we have used the HTTPS adapter and tested the IFlow using Postman. Based on the requirements, this can be changed - for example, the JMS or ProcessDirect adapters can also be used instead of the HTTPS adapter.

Next comes the key part. In the Content Modifier, I have explicitly defined the prompt that will be sent to the AI model.

AI Model Prompt Content Payload

You can customize the prompt based on your requirements. Now, let’s look at the AI Adapter and the configuration required for the Custom OpenAI REST variant.

In the Connection tab:

  • Address: Use the base URL of the endpoint.
  • API Key Alias: Specify the secure parameter created in the Security Material. This secure parameter stores the API key for the AI model.

AI Adapter - Connection Settings

In the Processing tab configuration, since I’m not using an OpenAI model like ChatGPT—which provides standard endpoints such as /chat/completions or /responses (we’ll look at this later)—I have selected the Advanced type. This option provides more configuration parameters compared to the Basic type.

  1. HTTP Method – Since we are triggering the process to create an incident, select the POST method.

  2. Relative URL – Copy the relative path from the actual endpoint URL and paste it here:

    /v1beta/models/gemini-flash-latest:generateContent

  3. Query – This is mostly used with the GET method. In our case, it is not applicable, so leave it as the default.

Headers

I created header parameters in the Content Modifier named X-goog-api-key to pass the API key and also added Content-Type: application/json.

Content Modifier Header Properties

Processing Tab:

AI Adapter - Advanced Configuration Type Settings

Let’s also discuss the Basic configuration type.

AI Adapter Basic Configuration Type Setting

 

 Parameter NameDescription
Configuration TypeDetermines how the AI adapter is configured. Basic allows configuration through predefined UI fields such as model and prompt, while Advanced provides more flexibility to define the entire request payload manually.
CategorySpecifies the type of AI capability to be used. Available options include Chat Completions, Responses, and Platform. Each category represents a different set of AI APIs used for conversational AI, structured responses, or advanced platform capabilities such as embeddings and image generation.
Entity TypeRepresents the specific AI resource within the selected category. 
Operation

Defines the action to be executed using the selected entity. The available operations depend on the selected Category and Entity Type.

For example, in Chat Completions, the operation is typically Create a model response for a given conversation. Other entities in the platform category may support operations like create, retrieve, update, or delete resources.

Request Payload Source

Determines how the request payload (prompt and parameters) is passed to the AI model.

Options include:

1. UI Configurable – Allows the prompt and model to be defined directly in the adapter configuration.

2. Exchange Body – Allows the request payload to be dynamically provided from the integration flow, typically using a Content Modifier or Groovy script, and passed as the message body.

Model (when UI Configurable is selected)

Specifies the AI model used to generate the response. The list contains the models available in the configured AI service (for example, GPT-based models).

Prompt (when UI Configurable is selected)

Allows the user to directly define the prompt or instruction that will be sent to the AI model. It can include instructions, questions, or contextual information required for the AI interaction.

Difference between /chat/completions and /response endpoints in OpenAI API

Feature/chat/completions/responses

Purpose

Designed specifically for chat-based interactionsA unified endpoint for all types of AI responses
Input FormatUses messages array (system, user, assistant)Uses a more flexible input structure
Future scopeOlder approach (still supported)Recommended modern API by OpenAI
FunctionalitiesPrimarily text/chatSupports text, images, audio, and tool use

Example:

1. /chat/completions

POST /v1/chat/completions
{
  "model":"gpt-4o-mini",
  "messages": [
    {"role":"system", "content":"You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role":"user", "content":"Explain SAP CPI in simple terms"}
  ]
}

2. /responses

POST /v1/responses
{
  "model":"gpt-4.1-mini",
  "input":"Explain SAP CPI in simple terms"
}

Key Takeaway

  • /chat/completions → Built specifically for chat-style conversations.
  • /responses → A new unified API that works for multiple AI capabilities and is the recommended approach for new integrations.

As the next step, I created a Groovy script to process the response from the AI and extract and format the relevant content required. If you look at my prompt, I asked the model to return the response in JSON format. However, inside the Content tag, the model returned something like this:

{
  "candidates": [
    {
      "content": {
        "parts": [
          {
            "text": "```json\n{\n  \"ErrorDescription\": \"Access denied due to missing or invalid authentication credentials for the requested resource.\",\n  \"PossibleCauses\": \"Invalid or incorrect username/password was provided; The authentication token or API key is missing; The provided token has expired; The user account is locked or disabled; Insufficient permissions for accessing the resource; Server-side authentication configurations are incorrect; Network issues prevented sending valid credentials.\",\n  \"ResolutionSteps\": \"Verify your username and password are correct; Ensure the authentication token or API key is present and valid; Generate a new authentication token if it has expired; Confirm you are using the correct authentication method; Contact your administrator to check account status and permissions; Review server logs for authentication configuration issues; Check network connectivity to the authentication provider; Clear your browser cache and cookies, then retry the action.\",\n  \"SupportGuidance\": \"If the issue persists after following the resolution steps, please contact your IT support team. Provide them with the exact error message, time of occurrence, and any steps you have already attempted. This information will help expedite the investigation.\",\n  \"IncidentMetadata\": {\n    \"state\": \"In Progress\",\n    \"incident_state\": \"Investigating\",\n    \"impact\": \"2\"\n  }\n}\n```"
          }
        ],
        "role": "model"
      },
      "finishReason": "STOP",
      "index": 0
    }
  ],
  "usageMetadata": {
    "promptTokenCount": 257,
    "candidatesTokenCount": 266,
    "totalTokenCount": 1366,
    "promptTokensDetails": [
      {
        "modality": "TEXT",
        "tokenCount": 257
      }
    ],
    "thoughtsTokenCount": 843
  },
  "modelVersion": "gemini-2.5-flash",
  "responseId": "XvevabT6KcDcqtsP_8GP8Q0"
}

Next, I used a Groovy script to extract only the JSON content present inside the text tag. The output after the Groovy script will contain only the required JSON content. Now, we can use the JSON to XML Converter to transform the below payload into an XML structure.

{
  "ErrorDescription": "Access denied due to missing or invalid authentication credentials for the requested resource.",
  "PossibleCauses": "Invalid or incorrect username/password was provided; The authentication token or API key is missing; The provided token has expired; The user account is locked or disabled; Insufficient permissions for accessing the resource; Server-side authentication configurations are incorrect; Network issues prevented sending valid credentials.",
  "ResolutionSteps": "Verify your username and password are correct; Ensure the authentication token or API key is present and valid; Generate a new authentication token if it has expired; Confirm you are using the correct authentication method; Contact your administrator to check account status and permissions; Review server logs for authentication configuration issues; Check network connectivity to the authentication provider; Clear your browser cache and cookies, then retry the action.",
  "SupportGuidance": "If the issue persists after following the resolution steps, please contact your IT support team. Provide them with the exact error message, time of occurrence, and any steps you have already attempted. This information will help expedite the investigation.",
  "IncidentMetadata": {
    "state": "In Progress",
    "incident_state": "Investigating",
    "impact": "2"
  }
}

In the next Content Modifier, I created a few properties with the Source Type set to XPath. These properties are then used to construct the request payload that will be sent to ServiceNow.

Custom Properties created in Content Modifier

Content Modifier Request Payload for ServiceNow Ticket Creation

Content Modifier Body:

{
    "short_description": "${property.short_description}",
    "description": "${property.PossibleCauses}, ${property.ResolutionSteps}, ${property.SupportGuidance}",
    "state": "${property.state}",
    "incident_state": "${property.incident_state}",
    "impact": "${property.impact}"
}

Finally, let’s look at the ServiceNow Receiver Adapter.

In the Connection tab:

  • Address: Copy and paste your ServiceNow instance URL.
  • Authentication: Select Basic Authentication.
  • Credentials: Enter the user credentials that you created in the Security Material.

ServiceNow Connection Tab Settings

In the Processing tab, we are presented with several additional parameters to configure.

ServiceNow Adapter Processing Tab Configurations

API Version - You can choose from different REST endpoint versions. If you are not certain which version to use, it is recommended to leave it as the default value.

Operation - Several operations are available. Since we are creating an incident, select the Create Object operation.

Table Name - Incident because that were all the ticket are stored in ServiceNow

Display Values - The Display Value option determines how reference field values are returned in the response payload. 

In ServiceNow tables, many fields store internal system identifiers (Sys IDs) instead of human-readable values. The Display Value setting controls whether the adapter returns:

  • the raw system value,
  • the human-readable display value, or
  • both the system value and display value.

Access Data Across Domain - This option allows the adapter to retrieve records from multiple domains within a ServiceNow instance.

  • If disabled, the adapter accesses data only from the current domain.

  • If enabled, it can retrieve records from all available domains, such as Global, IT, or Infrastructure.

Response Field - Specifies the fields or properties to be returned in the ServiceNow response. If multiple fields are required, they should be separated using commas.

Render Response For - Available options are Both, Mobile, and Desktop. This setting determines how the response is formatted based on the client type, ensuring compatibility across different platforms.

Suppress System Fields Auto Generation - This option prevents ServiceNow from automatically generating certain system fields when a record is created. Examples include: sys_id, sys_created_on

Display Raw Values - When Display Raw Values is enabled, the API returns the exact value stored in the database. By default, ServiceNow returns data in a formatted or human-readable format.

Exclude Reference Link - In ServiceNow responses, reference fields often include a link to the related resource. When Exclude Reference Link is enabled, the response returns only the value and removes the reference link.

Let’s now look at the actual difference in the response when all these properties are enabled.

Response Without Enabling the Mentioned Fields

Response when the Mentioned Fields are enabled

This is how these options affect the ServiceNow response payload.

Under the Format section, we have Request and Response options. These are quite straightforward, so I’ll skip a detailed explanation here.

Note: The available properties may vary depending on the selected operation. For more details about the ServiceNow Receiver Adapter, refer to the official documentation. ServiceNow Receiver Adapter | SAP Help Portal

Important Note: Make sure your ServiceNow instance is active and running. If the instance is in sleep mode while testing the IFlow, you may receive a response such as “Instance Hibernating Page.”

End to End testing from Postman

In the first Content Modifier, the error tag is captured and stored in a property. In the message body, the expression ${property.errorMsg} is used so that the prompt dynamically includes the specific error content. This ensures that for each error, the AI model receives a different prompt based on the error message.

Response: 

You can see an incident is created with a number "INC0010027". Now, let’s check ServiceNow to see where the incident is created. As expected, a new entry appears in the incident table.

Conclusion:

By integrating the AI Adapter with ServiceNow in SAP Integration Suite, we can significantly improve how incidents are identified and created within enterprise landscapes. Instead of manually analyzing logs and raising tickets, the integration flow can capture errors, send them to an AI model for analysis, and automatically generate a structured incident in ServiceNow.

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Using the Python SDK for SAP Data Tasks (Part 2 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 2 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  GENERATIVE AI HUB

SAP Generative AI Hub: Using the Python SDK for SAP Data Tasks

Five practical SAP use cases with working Python code — across SAP AI Launchpad notebooks, local scripts, and BTP applications — using the generative-ai-hub-sdk for clean, production-ready authentication.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 2 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Once your Generative AI Hub is configured (see Part 1), the generative-ai-hub-sdk Python package handles OAuth token management automatically — removing the biggest friction point from direct API integration. This article covers SDK installation and configuration across three environments (AI Launchpad notebooks, local Python scripts, BTP applications), followed by five working code examples for real SAP data tasks: exception log summarization, HANA query analysis, ABAP/SQL code generation, master data classification, and structured data extraction from SAP documents.

Series Context

This is Part 2 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Part 1 covered the complete setup path from BTP entitlements to your first API call. If you haven't configured your AI Core instance and resource group yet, start there before continuing.

This part focuses on practical Python integration — the code patterns you'll use for real SAP workloads, across the three environments where SAP practitioners most commonly run Python against the Hub.

 

Environment

Best For

Key Consideration

AI Launchpad Notebooks

Interactive development, rapid prototyping

Credentials pre-configured — fastest to start

Local Python Script

Automation, scheduled jobs, local development

Requires .env file with service key credentials

BTP Application

Production workloads, CAP integration, APIs

Credentials via BTP environment variables or secrets

SDK INSTALLATION & CONFIGURATION 

Installing and Configuring the generative-ai-hub-sdk

Why the SDK Over Direct API Calls

Part 1 showed the raw OAuth 2.0 flow using direct API calls — which is useful to understand what's happening under the hood. For any real workload, the generative-ai-hub-sdk is the better path. It handles token acquisition, token refresh, retry logic, and deployment ID resolution automatically. Your application code focuses on the task, not the authentication plumbing.

Installation

# Install the SAP Generative AI Hub SDK
pip install generative-ai-hub-sdk
 
# Optional but recommended — for HANA connection examples later
pip install hdbcli pandas python-dotenv

Configuration: AI Launchpad Notebooks

  ▶  Environment: SAP AI Launchpad Notebooks 

When running inside SAP AI Launchpad notebooks, the SDK automatically picks up credentials from the notebook environment. No configuration file is needed — this is the fastest way to start experimenting.

# In AI Launchpad notebooks — credentials are pre-injected
# No .aicore-config.json needed, just import and use
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import OpenAI
 
# The SDK resolves the connection automatically
client = OpenAI()

Configuration: Local Python Script

  ▶  Environment: Local Python Script 

For local development, create a configuration file that points the SDK to your AI Core service key credentials. Store this as .aicore-config.json in your home directory or reference it via an environment variable.

environment variable.
# ~/.aicore-config.json — from your AI Core service key
{
  "AICORE_AUTH_URL": "https://<your-subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_ID": "<clientid from service key>",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET": "<clientsecret from service key>",
  "AICORE_BASE_URL": "<AI_API_URL from service key>",
  "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP": "default"
}

Never commit .aicore-config.json to version control. Add it to your .gitignore immediately. For team environments, use environment variables (AICORE_AUTH_URL, AICORE_CLIENT_ID, etc.) instead of the config file — the SDK reads both patterns automatically.

Configuration: BTP Application

  ▶  Environment: BTP Application 

In BTP applications, bind the AI Core service instance to your application. The SDK reads credentials from the VCAP_SERVICES environment variable that BTP injects automatically at runtime — no manual credential management required.

# BTP injects VCAP_SERVICES automatically when AI Core is bound
# The SDK reads it without any additional configuration
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client
 
# SDK auto-resolves from VCAP_SERVICES in BTP runtime
proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')

Once credentials are configured for your target environment, all five use case examples below work identically across all three environments. The SDK abstracts the credential source — your application code does not change.

FIVE SAP USE CASES WITH WORKING CODE 

SAP Data Tasks: Working Code Examples

The following examples cover five practical SAP use cases. Each uses the same SDK pattern — choose your preferred model by changing one parameter. All examples use Claude 3.5 Sonnet as the default; swap the model_name value for GPT-4o, Gemini, or Mistral as needed based on the model comparison in this week's LinkedIn post.

 

Use Case

What the Model Does

Exception log summarization

Parse and summarize SAP SM21 / application error logs into structured root cause analysis

HANA query result analysis

Interpret query output and surface anomalies, trends, or data quality issues

ABAP / SQL / HANA code generation

Generate or explain code from natural language descriptions

Master data classification

Tag and categorize SAP master data records against defined classification schemes

Structured data extraction

Pull structured fields from SAP documents, change logs, or transport descriptions

Use Case 1: Summarizing SAP Exception Logs

SAP exception logs — SM21 system logs, application error dumps, job logs — are high-volume and time-consuming to triage manually. This pattern feeds raw log text to the model and returns a structured root cause summary with recommended actions.

# Use Case 1: SAP Exception Log Summarization
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
log_text = '''
  [SM21 Log Extract]
  E  RABAX  INCLUDE: ZREPORT_DELTA_LOAD Line 847
  E  Runtime Error: COMPUTE_INT_PLUS_OVERFLOW
  E  Date: 2026-03-10  Time: 04:32:17
  E  Work process terminated abnormally
'''
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP Basis and ABAP expert.
  Analyze SAP exception logs and return:
  1. Root cause (one sentence)
  2. Affected component
  3. Severity: Critical / High / Medium / Low
  4. Recommended action
  Be concise and technical.'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Analyze this SAP log:\n{log_text}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
print(response.content)

Output example — Root cause: Integer overflow in delta load procedure at ZREPORT_DELTA_LOAD line 847 due to 32-bit counter exceeding maximum value. Component: ABAP Runtime. Severity: Critical. Action: Cast counter variable to BIGINT and redeploy procedure.

Use Case 2: Analyzing HANA Query Results

This pattern connects to HANA Cloud, executes a query, and passes the result set to the model for interpretation — surfacing anomalies, trends, or data quality issues that would otherwise require manual review.

# Use Case 2: HANA Query Result Analysis
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import pandas as pd
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
# Connect to HANA Cloud
conn = hdb.connect(
    address='<your-hana-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com',
    port=443,
    user='<user>',
    password='<password>',
    encrypt=True
)
 
# Run your query and load into DataFrame
query = '''
  SELECT PLANT, MATERIAL, SUM(QUANTITY) AS TOTAL_QTY,
         COUNT(*) AS RECORD_COUNT
  FROM STOCK_OVERVIEW
  GROUP BY PLANT, MATERIAL
  ORDER BY TOTAL_QTY DESC
  LIMIT 50
'''
df = pd.read_sql(query, conn)
 
# Pass result to model for analysis
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='You are an SAP data analyst. Identify anomalies, trends, and data quality issues in SAP query results. Be specific and reference actual values from the data.'),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Analyze this SAP stock query result:\n{df.to_string()}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
print(response.content)

Do not pass full production datasets to the model. Apply LIMIT clauses and aggregate before sending. For sensitive SAP data, review your BTP data residency settings and confirm model data handling aligns with your organization's data governance policy before deploying to production.

Use Case 3: Generating ABAP / SQL / HANA Code from Natural Language

This pattern accepts a plain-language description of a requirement and returns production-ready ABAP, SQL, or HANA SQL code. Useful for accelerating development, explaining existing code, or onboarding team members to unfamiliar syntax.

# Use Case 3: SAP Code Generation from Natural Language
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
# GPT-4o recommended for ABAP — see model comparison article
 
requirement = '''
  Write a HANA SQL procedure that:
  - Reads from a virtual table SOURCE_SCHEMA.ORDERS
  - Filters records where CHANGE_DATE > last_run_date parameter
  - Inserts new records into TARGET_SCHEMA.ORDERS_DELTA
  - Updates a control table DELTA_CONTROL with the new last_run_date
  - Handles errors with a rollback
'''
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='''You are a senior SAP HANA SQL developer.
  Generate clean, production-ready HANA SQL code.
  Include comments explaining key sections.
  Flag any HANA-specific considerations or limitations.'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Generate HANA SQL for this requirement:\n{requirement}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
print(response.content)

 

Use Case 4: Classifying SAP Master Data

Master data classification — tagging materials, vendors, cost centers, or GL accounts against defined schemes — is high-volume and rule-heavy. This pattern uses the model to apply a classification schema to a batch of records and return structured JSON output ready for loading back into SAP.

# Use Case 4: SAP Master Data Classification
import json
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
materials = [
  {'MATNR': '000000000010001234', 'MAKTX': 'Hydraulic Pump Assembly 250bar'},
  {'MATNR': '000000000010005678', 'MAKTX': 'Office Chair Ergonomic Mesh Black'},
  {'MATNR': '000000000010009012', 'MAKTX': 'SAP S4HANA License Annual Renewal'},
]
 
classification_schema = ['Mechanical Components', 'Office Supplies',
                         'Software & Licenses', 'Raw Materials', 'Services']
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content=f'''Classify SAP material master records.
  Return ONLY valid JSON array. No explanation.
  Schema: {classification_schema}
  Format: [{{'MATNR': '...', 'CATEGORY': '...', 'CONFIDENCE': 'High/Medium/Low'}}]'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Classify these materials:\n{json.dumps(materials)}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
classified = json.loads(response.content)
print(json.dumps(classified, indent=2))

Prompt the model to return only JSON with no preamble or explanation when you need structured output for downstream processing. Claude is particularly consistent at following this instruction on classification tasks.

Use Case 5: Extracting Structured Data from SAP Documents

SAP landscapes accumulate semi-structured text in transport request descriptions, change log comments, functional spec attachments, and ticket notes. This pattern extracts structured fields from unstructured SAP document text — turning narrative descriptions into queryable data.

# Use Case 5: Structured Data Extraction from SAP Documents
import json
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
transport_description = '''
  TR: DEVK912345 - FI Delta Load Fix
  Developer: firstname.lastname@company.com
  Changed ZFI_DELTA_LOAD procedure to fix integer overflow on
  BKPF document counter. Root cause was 32-bit INT hitting max
  value after 2.1B records. Cast to BIGINT. Tested on DEV and QA.
  Affected tables: BKPF, BSEG. No customizing changes.
  Approved by: firstname1.lastname1@company.com on 2026-03-08
'''
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='''Extract structured data from SAP transport descriptions.
  Return ONLY valid JSON. No explanation.
  Fields: transport_id, title, developer, approver, approval_date,
  affected_tables (list), root_cause, change_type, tested_systems (list)'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Extract fields from:\n{transport_description}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
extracted = json.loads(response.content)
print(json.dumps(extracted, indent=2))

Choosing the Right Model per Use Case

Based on practical testing across these five patterns — and detailed in this week's LinkedIn model comparison post — here are the recommended model defaults:

 

Use Case

What the Model Does

Exception log summarization

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — consistent on long, noisy log text

HANA query result analysis

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — structured output quality, handles wide DataFrames

ABAP / SQL / HANA code generation

GPT-4o — broader SAP code knowledge, stronger on ABAP specifics

Master data classification

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — reliable JSON-only output, consistent confidence scoring

Structured data extraction

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — long-form document coherence, clean JSON output

Swap model_name='gpt-4o' or model_name='mistral-large' in any example to compare outputs. The SDK pattern is identical across all models.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 3: Building Your First Prompt Pipeline — chaining prompts, managing context, structured outputs for multi-step SAP workflows
  • Part 4: Connecting to HANA Cloud Vector Store — embedding generation, REAL_VECTOR storage, RAG on your SAP data
  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI in your SAP extension layer
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by Generative AI Hub

If you work through any of these examples and run into environment-specific issues — particularly around BTP binding or AI Launchpad notebook credential resolution — drop a comment below. The configuration edge cases are where community knowledge adds the most value.
